data.title: EPAM Systems: Navigating Post-Bubble Meltdown With Covered Calls
data.description:
Our recent trade in EPAM Systems Inc. (EPAM) is one that hits many different facets of our investing process. We will go over this company today from that lens and tell you what trade we placed in 2025.

The Company

EPAM describes its business in the following way.

We are consultants, designers, engineers, architects and trainers who enable our clients to be competitive and disruptive in the marketplace through innovative technology solutions, while helping them to successfully navigate through multiple waves of technology and social change

Source: EPAM

To expand on that, we can add that it operates at the intersection of 3 main areas.

1) Engineering: Building scalable, cloud-native software products, platforms, and complex enterprise systems.

2) Consulting: Providing business strategy, experience design, and technology consulting to help companies innovate.

3) Data & AI: Developing advanced data platforms and AI-driven solutions.

With all that said, let's focus on the different facets of our investing process that led us to EPA.

Technology Is Usually Not Our Bread & Butter

We have generally been weary of the technology sector at the current valuations. Even outside the recent extreme, technology is not one where we spend a lot of time. Our focus is always on income generation using defensive entry points and technology companies don't always enter into that arena. We prefer a solid dividend yield from the company and most technology companies would rather spend that cash on growth. But EPAM was different in a few ways and that got us to monitoring it as it got slammed in 2025.

Valuation Over All Else

Yes, we generally don't go into technology but there was something just majestically poetic about seeing a bubble fully implode. We couldn't take our eyes off it. EPAM was down close to 80% (when we made the trade), versus the Invesco QQQ Trust Inc. (QQQ) being up 59%. Sure, we are measuring from the absolute peak in EPAM price near January 1, 2022, but it must have been still extremely painful for any EPAM holder, whenever they bought in.

That of course is just price. What happened to the company's revenues? They grew by 41%. Investors tend to look at us with disbelief when we make claims that stocks can drop 70%-80% with a strong rise of revenues, simply because their valuations are so horrible. EPAM is a textbook case of that.

Of course that revenue growth combined with the big drop in price over this timeframe, worked wonders for EPAM's price to sales ratio.

To show our real time perspective, here is EPAM's price to sales ratio the day we went long.

It is not like EPAM's profits were impacted anywhere in this timeframe. EV to EBITDA had dropped from 60X to around 12X on September 30, 2025. Today it is still a very reasonable 16X.

In a nutshell, EPAM had some issues with the Russian invasion of Ukraine which displaced some of its workforce. It also slowed its growth rate. When you pay 12X sales, it is a matter of time before something comes and slaps you in the face. Any surprise can be a negative surprise. The last drop in 2025 was created with the thought process that LLMs can replace all consultancies and EPAM's business is on the endangered species list. We did not think that was the case and we also felt, EPAM had the muscle to fight any challenges. Alongside that, it had a rock-solid balance sheet with current assets exceeding total liabilities by $1.4 billion.

That cash is very relevant for a firm of this size.

What Got Us To Buy

EPAM was cheapish, at $151 per share. A lot of bulls might say it was the best thing since sliced bread as you can't get a growing technology company at under 13X forward earnings. This brings us to another problem we have with technology companies. They really abuse the stock based compensation model and EPAM was no different. You can see just all the addbacks to GAAP earnings that get us to the adjusted number. $3.13 of stock based compensation per share (the next two lines are just a breakdown of that).

There are some other big addbacks that don't really make us happy. The way we saw it was that after subtracting the net cash, we were getting the stock at about 17X real earnings.

Not exactly a bargain, but perhaps a bargain for the technology sector. The company was also supporting its stock price with a vigor for the first time.

EPAM had also become a crowded short trade (at least relative to its history).

All of that got us, "if we believe AI is a bubble, then EPAM is likely underpriced because of the fear". But even at $151.00, net of the cash, we still felt the margin of safety was low. Sure, we could add a fair value much higher up, but stocks do get drastically undervalued at the end of bubbles. Look at PayPal (PYPL) trading at single digit forward earnings as an example. Fortunately for us, we have the most important tool in the toolkit. Long dated calls. Unlike the blind, front-month, covered call selling that most buy-write funds do, we tend to look at the long end of the curve. In this case EPAM's longest dated calls that were available were for December 2026. The premiums were stupidly high and we were getting a 23.25% annualized return, if EPAM was just flat over 15 months.

It also got our net cash outlay to $117.00 per share and if you adjust that for the net cash held, you are looking at about $100 per share for the business. That we could live with.

Verdict

As it stands, EPAM has jumped 40% from our entry point. The company has asserted stronger growth ahead and the bears are throwing in the towel. A straight buy would have obviously been the better choice with perfect hindsight. But we are never ready to compromise on valuation and entry points. We are long the covered call setup and plan to hang on to see how this evolves in 2026.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: JPMorgan Chase: Earnings Show Lower Interest Rates Helping Bank (Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier today, JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) reported its fourth quarter earnings. The nation’s largest bank by loans and deposits, JPMorgan Chase is a bellwether that will set the tone for bank earnings across the industry. I last discussed the bank from the perspective of its preferred shares back in July. Today, I believe JPMorgan Chase has benefited from lower interest rates and will continue to do so throughout 2026, making the bank’s shares a Buy.

How Have Interest Rate Trends Affected the Bank?

Lower interest rates have had a stimulating effect on JPMorgan Chase. While the bank’s asset yield resumed its decline in the fourth quarter (dropping 10 basis points to 4.95%), the bank’s borrowing yield also declined by a more accelerated 19 basis points, to 2.94%. This allowed the net interest spread and the net yield to increase by 9 basis points. The net yield, which is a broader measure, now sits at 2.54%, and has increased for two consecutive quarters, reversing a general decline that started in the fourth quarter of 2023.

Just as the yields have trended, so has interest income and expense. Interest income in the fourth quarter fell by $600 million to $48.8 billion, marking the first quarterly decline since the first quarter. Interest expense also fell, and by more than interest income at over $1.5 billion to $23.8 billion. This was also the first quarter to quarter decline in interest expense since the first quarter.

The Federal Reserve’s continuation of interest rate declines is certainly an influence to the bank’s changes in interest income and expense. Fortunately, the increased drop in interest expense has allowed net interest income (interest income less interest expense) to rise by $1 billion to just under $25 billion for the quarter. This is the highest recorded level of net interest income and $1.6 billion higher than in the fourth quarter a year ago.

Changes in Loans and Deposits

The growth in JPMorgan Chase’s balance sheet has allowed it to grow net interest income while simultaneously experiencing lower interest rates. In the fourth quarter, the bank experienced loan growth of 4.1%, the second largest quarterly growth level since the acquisition of First Republic Bank. On a year-over-year basis, loans grew by just under 11%.

The deposit side of the balance sheet was slightly tamer but still had growth. Deposits grew by 0.4% during the fourth quarter and by 6.4% on a year over year basis. While quarterly deposit growth was slow, deposit growth for the year outpaced the commercial banking industry by more than 200 basis points.

For any bank, when loan growth outpaces deposit growth, it means that the loan to deposit ratio is rising. The loan to deposit ratio is important because it signals how much of the bank’s deposits are being used to finance loans. If the ratio is high, the bank may need to rely on external financing, which can increase interest expense and put pressure on net interest income.

For JPMorgan Chase, the loan to deposit ratio is now at the highest level the bank has seen since 2019. Fortunately, at 57%, the ratio is below industry benchmarks and still reasonable. The bank’s external financing, which is influenced by its investment bank, did ease back in the fourth quarter, dropping by $122 billion to $942 billion. This was something I had become uncomfortable about earlier in the year, but the reduction of external financing should continue to provide a boost to net interest income in future quarters.

Risks to JPMorgan Chase

As with any bank, loan performance is the primary risk for investors to monitor. In the case of JPMorgan Chase, nonperforming assets (NPA) steadily rose throughout 2025, but declined by $300 million to $10.3 billion in the fourth quarter. The bank has an allowance for credit losses designed to protect earnings from loan losses. As of the end of the fourth quarter, the allowance for credit losses (1.73%) stood more than 100 basis points higher than the NPA ratio (0.69%), providing adequate coverage.

One other risk that should be monitored by investors throughout 2026 is interest rate risk. While the bank has benefited from rates declining, a sudden reversal in Fed policy and/or increase in short-term rates could have a punitive effect on net interest income. This is since out of the $942 billion in external financing, $442 billion is tied to short-term repo agreements and an additional $64 billion is in short-term borrowings. For the time being, the Fed is projecting lower interest rates, but this risk should be on the mind of investors if inflation re-emergences as a problem.

Conclusion

JPMorgan Chase is certainly capitalizing on the decline in interest rates. The bank has improved its net interest income and has reduced its external financing. JPM continues to maintain loan growth above the industry average and with declining nonperforming assets, profitability should continue to improve. Based on these improved performance measures and solid outlook, I believe JPMorgan shares would be a great addition to investors portfolio in 2026.

data.title: Bank of Montreal: Looking Well-Placed Headed Into 2026
data.description:
Diversification has been a nice crutch for Bank of Montreal (BMO) (BMO:CA) of late. While fiscal 2025 was a more pedestrian year for its bread-and-butter Canadian business, better trends in the Capital Markets, Wealth Management, and U.S. Banking segments, plus some help from buybacks, ultimately drove earnings per share around 25% higher. As you might expect, that's been great for the stock, which has returned nearly 30% since my opening piece last summer.

This is a challenging call. On the one hand, BMO has seen a clear degree of valuation uplift since I first wrote on it, so the shares haven't gotten intrinsically cheaper. On the other hand, BMO has lagged its domestic peers in that time, so you could argue the relative investment case has actually improved. While I will keep BMO as a “watch list” stock for now, readers with existing exposure to Canadian banks may consider this a name to rotate into.

Diversification And Capital Helped In 2025…

(All financial figures are in Canadian dollars unless stated otherwise)

Last year was a tumultuous one for the Canadian economy, and I doubt readers would be surprised to learn that BMO's domestic credit charges rose sharply. The bank set aside $2.36 billion to cover potential bad debt in its Canadian Personal and Commercial Banking segment last year, 42% more than in fiscal 2024. While the situation was healthier above this line (pre-provision income rose 7% last year), it wasn't quite enough to lift that unit's adjusted net income, which fell 4% to $3.35 billion. At 19.8%, the adjusted return on was still very healthy, though it was down 170 basis points compared to 2024.

This is where being a universal bank has its benefits. While BMO's domestic engine spluttered a little last year, its U.S. Banking, Wealth Management, and Capital Markets segments all delivered strong double-digit earnings growth. All in, BMO did $9.25 billion in adjusted net profit last year, up 24% on 2024. Buybacks kicked in an extra 145 basis points in terms of share-level growth, as the bank directed some of its excess capital back into investors' pockets.

… And Could Again In 2026

Clearly, 25% annual earnings growth will not be the long-term norm for BMO. That said, I still see more going right than wrong for the bank in 2026.

Let's start with the all-important Canadian P&C segment. Last year, provisioning on impaired loans ran at 58 basis points relative to total loans in this business, the highest print in years. Household mortgages account for around 45% of this unit's loans. While there isn't much loss content in that part of the book, BMO has had to set aside more money to cover things like credit card and business lending.

Now, gross impaired loans have continued to tick up throughout this period. 0.86% of BMO's Canadian loans were impaired at the end of its fourth quarter, up from 0.64% at the start of the year.

Positively, some macro trends have started to brighten up since the end of that period—including within the Canadian jobs report, with Canada adding nearly 130,000 jobs in the last three months of the calendar year, more than across the whole of the first nine months. Meanwhile, at 6.8%, the county's unemployment rate is around 30 basis points off last year's peak. Last year, higher provisioning in the Canadian P&C business was a $705 million year-on-year pre-tax headwind to BMO. If that line can simply stabilize in 2026, it'll help.

That's important because there are tailwinds elsewhere in BMO's business. Ironically, it's the weakest part of the group that I am most bullish about: the U.S. Banking segment. That business chipped in roughly $3.1 billion in net income last year, 34% more than in 2024. In contrast to the Canadian P&C segment, U.S. Banking's credit cycle probably bottomed in BMO's fiscal 2024. Credit charges were $1.68 billion in that year, falling to $1.04 billion in 2025. Sequential trends have also been favorable, with credit expenses falling for four straight quarters.

Despite this, U.S. Banking's profitability remains weak. This unit's adjusted return on equity was only 8.1% last year, nearly 400 basis points below management's 12% target. However, given improving credit costs, 2025's earnings and ROE were weighted more toward the last two quarters. Assuming a full year of cleaner credit in fiscal 2026, that business could be a source of low-hanging fruit for BMO.

The outlook for some of BMO's non-banking businesses looks decent as well. For instance, in addition to managing and/or administering the assets of its wealth management clients, BMO is also one of the largest ETF providers in Canada. It also owns InvestorLine, a self-directed broker for retail investors. In total, BMO currently has $390 billion in assets under management and $283 billion in assets under administration in its Wealth segment.

As you can imagine, a chunk of these assets are in equity index trackers. Buoyant financial markets have been good for this business, lifting asset-based fees. Last year, BMO booked just over $1 billion in net profit in its Wealth and Asset Management subdivision, nearly 30% more than in 2024. Positively, markets seem to have gotten off to a decent start in the firm's fiscal 2026, which should help support asset levels and fees.

Then there's the capital position to consider. BMO ended last year with a 13.3% ratio of Common Equity Tier 1 to risk-weighted assets, 80 basis points above management's target. Given it had $438 billion in RWAs, this would imply around $3.5 billion in surplus capital, equal to nearly 3% of BMO's current market cap.

This cushion is allowing BMO to supplement its regular distributions with buybacks. In addition to paying out over 50% of last year's profits via dividends, it also retired 3% of its outstanding shares. The bank is now earning a healthy 2%-plus return on its risk-weighted assets, giving it plenty of scope to keep topping up dividends with buybacks, fund organic growth, and maintain its capital ratio near management's target. The buyback element will naturally contribute positively to EPS growth.

Valuation

Given the above, I do think BMO is well placed heading into 2026. However, valuation matters in investing, and this is where I think things get a little more challenging, as the stock has gained over 25% since my initial piece. Back then, BMO was trading for 1.37 times its book value—which was in line with its ten-year average. This has since risen to 1.67 times.

BMO's dividend yield has been on a similar journey. It was 4.4% when I first covered the stock, also in line with its ten-year average. It has since fallen to 3.6%.

While the above would suggest that BMO is not intrinsically cheap, it is important to point out that the stock has actually lagged its peers recently. Indeed, while BMO has seen a 22% uplift in its price-to-book ratio since my opening piece, Royal Bank of Canada (RY), Toronto-Dominion (TD), Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce (CM), and Bank of Nova Scotia (BNS) have seen 28%-32% moves in their respective multiples in that time. That being the case, investors with existing exposure to Canadian banks may wish to consider rotating into BMO, though I remain “Neutral” for now.

data.title: Franklin Small Cap Growth Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: US equities delivered solid gains over the fourth quarter of 2025, maintaining their upward trajectory against a backdrop of generally robust corporate earnings, despite some mixed economic signals and investor concerns about stretched valuations in technology-related stocks. Continued monetary easing by the US Federal Reserve (Fed), along with a constructive shift in US–China relations, also underpinned investor sentiment. The outperformance of the Magnificent Seven mega-capitalization technology stocks during the quarter boosted returns for the S&P 500 Index and the Nasdaq Composite Index, while the relatively strong performance of the Dow Jones Industrial Average reflected the enduring strength of blue-chip companies. By investment style, value investing surpassed growth in the large-, mid- and small-capitalization tiers, with large-cap stocks performing better than their mid- and small-cap counterparts.
  • Contributors: For the quarter, stock selection decisions factored into the fund’s relative outperformance. Strong stock selection bolstered relative results in the information technology (IT) sector. Stock selection and an underweight in the industrials sector and stock selection in the energy sector also contributed to a lesser extent.
  • Detractors: Stock selection and an overweight in the consumer staples sector detracted most from relative performance in the fourth quarter. An underweight in the health care sector and stock selection and an underweight in the real estate sector also proved adverse for relative returns.
  • Outlook: The performance of small-cap stocks in general was positive for the fourth quarter and for 2025, supported by broadly favorable valuations, earnings growth and monetary policy. Future performance is likely to depend on the health of the US economy, Fed decisions and overall investor confidence. We believe small-cap growth stocks are attractively valued, both relative to large caps and in light of their own underlying growth potential.

Performance Review

  • The fund’s return (Advisor Class without sales charges) was positive and meaningfully outperformed that of the Russell 2000 Growth Index. In the IT sector, Lumentum Holdings was a top contributor to returns. The photonics specialist reported solid fiscal first-quarter 2026 financial results, but it was the company’s second-quarter guidance of more than 20% sequential growth that rallied the stock. Increased artificial intelligence (AI) demand benefited its core business, including transceiver components and data center interconnects.
  • Olema Pharmaceuticals (OLMA) was a leading contributor in the health care sector. Shares of the clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company jumped after a competitor’s breast cancer drug trial succeeded, boosting confidence in Olema’s similar therapy. The company is a developer of targeted treatments for breast cancer.
  • In contrast, the health care sector was hindered by a position in uniQure (QURE) (not held by period-end), whose shares fell sharply after the US Food & Drug Administration withdrew its support for the firm’s Huntington’s disease trial data, raising regulatory uncertainty for the biotechnology company.

Outlook

  • As we enter 2026, our view on US equities remains positive but selective. In an environment where growth is available but not abundant, our focus is on owning high-quality franchises with durable competitive advantages, often supported by secular tailwinds.
  • In recent years, a small group of mega-capitalization innovators—dominated by the Magnificent Seven—has driven the majority of index returns, benefiting from scale and AI leadership. Looking toward 2026, we believe conditions should favor a broadening of equity market leadership, with opportunities emerging across sectors beyond IT and companies below the mega-cap threshold, which can offer an advantage for small- and mid-cap companies.
  • In 2026, we are focused on structural themes shaped by AI and automation, which are now producing measurable productivity gains not only in the IT sector but also in the health care, financials, industrials and energy sectors. We are also monitoring potential risks for the US economy, including inflation, regulatory pressures, geopolitical friction, infrastructure bottlenecks and unfavorable policy shifts.
  • Fiscal and economic conditions appear to be aligning to support growth. We expect continued innovation-led expansion underpinned by quality fundamentals in an environment where we think disciplined, active management has an advantage.

data.title: Apple: The Gemini Move May Lead To iPhone Upgrades And A New AI Distribution Model
data.description:
If strong iPhone 17 sales, despite no major Apple Intelligence upgrades, wasn't enough for the bulls on Apple Inc. (AAPL), the multiyear partnership to integrate Alphabet Inc.'s (GOOGL) Google Gemini into a revamped Siri later this year adds a second, more durable catalyst to the narrative.

I see two key revenue drivers from this partnership.

First, Apple Intelligence is still limited to iPhone 15 Pro and iPhone 16 models or newer, leaving a large portion of the installed base unable to access the feature set. If Gemini materially improves Siri’s usefulness, I see upside from a major upgrade cycle ahead.

That said, the momentum of the iPhone 17 makes me believe that users don't really care that much about AI features. This is a risk, given that Apple will be spending $1B a year for the Gemini integration.

The second pillar is related to AI distribution. As model providers improve monetization, Apple can replicate the default-search playbook by charging for default assistant/model placement. Given the September remedies ruling in the Google antitrust case, I believe there will be multiple credible bidders (Gemini, OpenAI (OPENAI), Anthropic (ANTHRO)) engaging in bidding wars.

At roughly 31x forward earnings after a 10% pullback, I reiterate my Strong Buy rating on Apple.

Could A Gemini-Powered Siri Become A 2026 iPhone Catalyst?

Let's start with the headlines.

Apple announced yesterday a multiyear partnership to integrate Google Gemini AI into a revamped Siri later this year. Unfortunately no concrete timeline (H1 vs. H2) was disclosed. I am not surprised in the slightest given the delays with Siri last year and, based on price action (Apple stock was relatively flat on Monday), I believe the market hasn't blinked either.

Furthermore, Google said Apple chose Gemini as the foundation for Apple’s next-gen foundation models, which will also power future Apple Intelligence features. I have a high conviction that the ChatGPT integration into Siri (which is an opt-in for complex queries since the end of 2024) will be replaced with Gemini. What does it mean for Google?

Well, as I said in my last Google coverage, a Gemini - ChatGPT crossover point is likely to happen this year (especially after Monday's partnership announcement):

Assuming Gemini maintains its 30% MAU growth rate (the same as in the period between August and November 2025) and OpenAI (OPENAI) its 5%-6% growth rate, I estimate the crossover point could be in roughly 12 months.

However, I'm here to talk about Apple. So, what's the upside for the iPhone maker?

First, Apple reiterated a key aspect of its architecture: Apple Intelligence will be designed to run on-device where possible. The Samsung Galaxy S25 family (in my view, iPhone's top competitor) is able to run Galaxy AI on-device. From a privacy perspective, this is a key feature. Now, for more complex tasks, Apple Intelligence will use Private Cloud Compute for more complex requests while still preserving the user's privacy. In other words, a Gemini-powered Siri does not automatically mean Google controls user data.

Never underestimate the value of privacy. In an age when every letter you type on a keyboard can be used to train frontier large language models, or LLMs, privacy could be the USP that convinces skeptical consumers (like me) to upgrade to a new phone.

So, what's the price tag for the Gemini's brain?

Neither Apple nor Google disclosed the exact amount. All we have is Bloomberg's whisper figure of $1B a year for a 1.2T parameter model.

How about the upside?

Here is where Apple hit the nail on its head. According to Apple Support, Apple Intelligence is only available for iPhone 15 Pro models and iPhone 16 models or newer.

The chart below is really interesting to understand the upside. It represents the top 10 models of iPhones for each week in the last year (in percentages) from 16M monthly active app users:

Notably, 52% of handset devices represent older models than the iPhone 15 Pro. Assuming 2B active devices, out of which, 1.5B could be iPhones, there could be 750 million iPhone devices that are incompatible with Apple Intelligence.

The upside could materialize as upgrades to newer, more expensive iPhone models. As seen below, on a TTM basis, iPhone revenue growth has been relatively modest over the past few years, despite the (unexpected) success of the iPhone 17.

That is, if (and only if) Gemini materially improves Siri usefulness (accuracy, context, and task completion). According to some sources, Siri handled about 1.5B requests per day in 2024. Even if we assume no growth since then (not a hard assumption, given the lack of upgrades), compared with an installed base of 2B+ active devices (~1.5B iPhones), I don’t think it’s a stretch to say a good chunk of iPhone users rely on Siri as a go-to feature they use daily.

Finally, a better Siri could be a tailwind for the services segment, which desperately needs to return back to mid to high teens of revenue growth.

If Siri becomes more useful, I believe it could increase time spent on handset device, which is historically correlated with consumer spending (and, part of that spending could be a tailwind for the services segment).

Another (Larger) Upside: AI Distribution

In the same way that Apple historically monetized default search placement (by getting paid big dollars to route high-intent user traffic to a search partner), I believe Apple could monetize AI queries.

Apple's moat is its massive installed base of 2B+ users.

The business model is relatively simple: a model provider (Gemini, ChatGPT, Claude, Mistral AI) pays Apple for being the default assistant model. In exchange, the model provider makes revenue from the user request (mainly through ads, although monetizing AI is a fragile topic at the moment).

The September remedies ruling in the Google antitrust case allows Google to keep the Apple deal and continue revenue-sharing payments. The only caveat is that the default-search placement agreements can only run for one year at a time and must be renegotiated/renewed annually. That's bad for Google and great for Apple, given that I expect a similar structure for the default LLM model in iPhone's devices. I count three large LLM providers (Gemini, ChatGPT, Claude) who would likely compete fiercely for being Apple's default LLM provider.

Now, let me clarify something. Yesterday's announcement says that the next generation of Apple foundation models will be based on Google’s Gemini models and cloud technology. That doesn’t imply Apple will start routing AI queries to Gemini (and getting paid for the placement).

At least, not yet. That will be the next leg of the growth story, once the likes of Gemini and ChatGPT figure out how to monetize their LLM chatbots. That’s when I expect bidding wars to break out to become Apple’s default LLM provider.

Valuation

Apple is trading at just 31x next year's earnings. The stock experienced a 10% haircut from the highs last month, and I see a key support level at $260.

Interestingly enough, Apple is trading at the same forward P/E multiple as Alphabet. Compared to the rest of the Mag 7 (ex-Tesla, as price action is not driven by fundamentals), the two stocks are in the #1 and #2 place in terms of the last 6-month returns.

Yet, I don't see their multiple significantly diverging from the Mag 7 group:

Overall, I believe valuation still makes sense, despite the outperformance in the past 6-months.

Risks

First, the unexpected success (see Tim Cook's remarks below) of the iPhone 17 is a double edged sword:

The iPhone 17 has been -- the family has been very well received there. We do believe that we'll return to growth in Q1, and that is largely based on the reception of the iPhone there. And so I couldn't be more pleased with how things are going there in the early going.

To me, the success of the iPhone 17 suggests that users are not concerned with AI features. Apple Intelligence may not be as desirable as the bulls think. If this is the case, any improvements to Siri could end up being a waste if users don't care about AI features.

As I said before, Apple was reported to pay Alphabet approximately $1B a year for the Gemini partnership. If an upgrade driven by better AI doesn't materialize, that billion a year will pressure the bottom line of the company.

The same goes for the AI distribution pillar. If users don't use AI search, Apple doesn't have that much of an edge as the bulls believe.

Overall, I believe the risks mentioned above are fat tail risks. Very small chance, in my view, but high impact.

Conclusion

I reiterate my Strong Buy rating on Apple, especially after the 10% cool off from the December highs.

The key factor to monitor is whether the Siri revamp (planned for later this year) actually ships without further slippage. Any delays could hurt the sentiment around the stock, given that the market is now pricing in several tailwinds from the Gemini partnership.

First, Apple Intelligence will need to deliver clear gains in accuracy, context, and task completion. If the upgrade in intelligence and usefulness is significant, the upside comes from a base of 750M iPhones (approximately 50% of total devices) that are incompatible with Apple Intelligence.

Second, if users adopt the new Apple Intelligence features, I believe Apple could become an AI distribution powerhouse. In the same way the company is charging for default search placements, I believe they will charge for default LLM providers. This could enable a new growth leg, once the LLM providers figure out how to monetize AI.

data.title: Fidelity D & D Bancorp: Stronger Fundamentals, Improving Rate Tailwinds, And A Long-Awaited Buy
data.description:
Since I first wrote about Fidelity D & D Bancorp, Inc. (FDBC), its stock price has risen only modestly, by nearly 1%, while its total shareholder returns (“TSR”) have increased by roughly 2%. This performance has trailed both the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and the iShares Russell 3000 ETF (IWV). In that initial article, I highlighted Fidelity’s solid fundamentals: steady loan and deposit growth, strong asset quality, and consistent, if rather unspectacular, earnings expansion. Nevertheless, investor sentiment remained cautious, and Fidelity’s shares continued to underperform despite what were, at the time, favorable economic conditions for banks. As a result, shareholders have experienced prolonged underperformance relative to both broad market indices and many peer institutions.

My original thesis was that investors should wait for clearer signs of momentum before establishing a position. Accordingly, I assigned a “Hold” rating. Six months later, Fidelity’s fundamentals have strengthened further, yet the stock’s muted gains suggest sentiment remains subdued. However, with an increasingly likely yield-curve steepening in 2026, a development that would disproportionately benefit regional banks, I am upgrading the rating to a “Buy.”

Profitability is Rising

Since that initial article, Fidelity reported its Q3 2025 results, with net income rising to $26.09 million on a trailing twelve-month (“TTM”) basis ended September 30, 2025, up from $20.79 million in the comparable prior-year period. This improvement was driven primarily by a $7.9 million increase in net interest income and a $0.97 million increase in non-interest income. President and Chief Executive Officer Daniel J. Santaniello characterized the quarter as delivering “very strong results,” paced by the effectiveness of the bank’s long-term, relationship-based strategy. He highlighted disciplined loan portfolio expansion, strong deposit growth, and improved yields on interest-earning assets as the key drivers of performance.

Profitability metrics have improved meaningfully. Return on average equity (“ROAE”) rose to 11.96% in Q3 2025, up from 10.57% a year earlier, representing the highest level since 2022. Return on assets (“ROA”) also improved to 0.97%, compared with 0.82% in the prior year. These gains have largely been driven by an expanding net interest margin ("NIM") and well-contained credit costs.

Prudent Management

Fully taxable equivalent (“FTE”) NIM increased by 22 basis points to 2.92% for the nine months ended September 30, 2025, from 2.70% in the prior-year period. This expansion reflects higher loan and lease yields combined with lower rates paid on interest-bearing deposits, underscoring the strength of Fidelity’s core deposit franchise and disciplined pricing strategy. The much lauded relationship-based deposit strategy and expense management have indeed led to expanding margins and improved earnings. Deposit growth has remained steady, with total deposits rising from $2.34 billion in 2024 to $2.45 billion on a TTM basis, allowing the bank to fund loan growth internally rather than relying on higher-cost wholesale funding.

Net loans increased to $1.89 billion in the TTM period from $1.78 billion in 2024, a notable achievement given the higher-rate environment and softer credit demand faced by many peers. Management has actively reallocated the balance sheet toward higher-yielding loans, including the sale of lower-yielding securities. Importantly, this growth has not come at the expense of credit quality. Management noted that,

“For the nine months ended September 30, 2025, the decrease in the provision for credit losses on loans and the provision for unfunded commitments compared to the prior year period was due to improved asset quality, specifically non-performing assets and non-accrual loans, compared to the same period in 2024.”

Non-performing assets (“NPAs”) declined to 0.11% of total assets from 0.29% a year earlier, while non-accrual loans stood at just 0.14% of total loans. Net charge-offs were negligible at 0.03% of average loans, placing Fidelity among the stronger community banks in terms of credit discipline. Fidelity remains among the top tier of community banks in credit discipline, with prudent underwriting and local market knowledge yielding a very conservative loan book.

Operating expenses have increased, but largely for growth-related reasons. Non-interest expense rose to $58.29 million in the TTM period from $55.54 million last year, reflecting investments in personnel and infrastructure to support business expansion. Despite this, efficiency has improved: the FTE efficiency ratio declined to 60.17% for the first nine months of 2025 from 65.48% a year earlier, suggesting that expense growth remains well controlled. Quite simply, it appears that Fidelity is spending to support growth, but that spending is disciplined.

A Well-Capitalized Bank

Fidelity remains well capitalized. Tier 1 capital stands at 9.27% of average assets, total risk-based capital at 14.52% of risk-weighted assets, and Tier 1 risk-based capital at 13.39%. Tangible book value per share increased from $31.98 in 2024 to $36.23 on a TTM basis, while tangible common equity rose to 7.69% of total assets.

Dividend growth continues to reflect management’s confidence in future earnings. Dividends per share increased from $1.14 in the first nine months of 2024 to $1.20 in the same period of 2025, and the board approved a 7.5% increase in the quarterly dividend to $0.43 for Q4 2025. This marks the bank’s 11th consecutive year of dividend growth and implies a forward dividend yield of 3.96% at the current share price.

Yield Curve Outlook Could Benefit Fidelity

While the yield curve remains inverted, expectations are building for a steepening cycle in 2026 as monetary policy eases under a new Federal Reserve chair. Juergen Michels of BayernLB forecasts a steepening of the yield curve from June 2026, with 2-year Treasury yields falling as deficits and Treasury issuance lead 10-year Treasury yields to an estimated 4.4% by year-end. This steepening, driven primarily by falling short-term rates rather than rising long-term yields, would be supportive of bank profitability by widening spreads without materially impairing credit demand. Regional banks such as Fidelity, which rely more heavily on traditional lending than larger, diversified institutions, are particularly sensitive to this dynamic. A more favorable rate environment could therefore act as a catalyst for both earnings growth and valuation expansion. Equity markets typically discount changes in the rate environment well in advance. As a result, even incremental shifts in expectations around monetary easing or curve normalization could support valuation expansion before the curve fully steepens.

Myra Thomas, a banking analyst at eMarketer, noted that,

"Simply put, a steeper yield curve is a vote of confidence in the economy. It translates to business and consumer optimism and a greater likelihood to borrow”.

Regional banks such as Fidelity are likely to benefit from this environment given that they are more reliant on lending compared to large banks, and this could lead to Fidelity enjoying a higher valuation this year.

Fidelity Remains Deeply Undervalued

At current levels, Fidelity trades at a price-to-book ratio of 1.09, below the sector median of 1.31 and Damodaran’s estimated regional bank average of 1.14. Assuming book value per share of $39.75 remains stable and the valuation multiple converges toward peer levels, a blended target price of approximately $48.69 implies upside of roughly 12% from current prices.

Risks

Looming large this year is the possibility of a deterioration in the macroeconomic environment that materially weakens credit quality or delays the anticipated yield-curve steepening. While Fidelity’s loan book is conservatively underwritten, a recession accompanied by rising unemployment or stress in commercial real estate could lead to higher delinquencies and increased provisions for credit losses.

Several mitigating factors reduce the severity of this risk. So far, the U.S. economy has proven resilient, with efficiency gains mitigating wage pressures. The economist Nouriel Roubini forecasts a

“growth recession (meaning below-trend GDP growth) for a few months, followed by a recovery and a gradual decline in the inflation rate toward the US Federal Reserve’s 2% target.”

Such a scenario would mitigate the risks of rising delinquencies.

Fidelity enters this period with exceptionally low non-performing assets, negligible net charge-offs, and a loan portfolio that has been actively de-risked through conservative underwriting and local-market discipline. These characteristics provide meaningful loss-absorption capacity even under a moderate downturn.

Secondly, if inflation proves more persistent than expected and policy rates remain higher for longer, funding costs could rise faster than asset yields, compressing NIMs. However, Fidelity’s reliance on relationship-based deposits and limited use of wholesale funding partially mitigates this risk by slowing deposit repricing relative to peers.

Conclusion

Fidelity’s financial performance continues to improve, with rising profitability, excellent asset quality, disciplined expense management, and strong capital levels. While investor sentiment remains cautious, the prospect of a more favorable rate environment in 2026 materially improves the risk-reward profile. Taken together, these factors justify upgrading the stock to a “Buy.”

data.title: ANI Pharmaceuticals: Strong 2025 Finish And Bullish 2026 Guidance
data.description:
Thesis: Bullish Guidance From Management

ANI Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (ANIP) has just reported a pretty strong finish to 2025 and the stock was up about 10% as a result. Management also outlined a very ambitious growth trajectory for 2026, which tells us they’re continuing their transformation into a leading Rare Disease-focused biopharmaceutical company. Preliminary, unaudited results show that the company achieved total net revenues of about $854 to $873 million, with an adjusted non-GAAP EBITDA figure in the range of $221 to $228 million. Now, this would represent significant year-over-year growth of at least 39% and 42%, respectively, which is why the market reaction was so positive.

As you know, their rare disease segment would mainly be driven by Purified Cortrophin Gel, which we saw deliver a very impressive performance, with full-year net revenues of around $347.8 million. This figure is up 76% from 2024. Elsewhere, Iluvien and Yutiq contributed about $74.9 million combined. Looking ahead, ANI expects 2026 total net revenues to fall somewhere in the range of $1.055 to $1.115 billion, with Cortrophin Gel alone projected to hit the $540 to $575 million mark. The guidance would represent a 60% increase in total revenues, which goes to show how focused ANI is now on those high-growth therapeutic areas. I made a table below to summarize all the figures from management in the update.

To capitalize on emerging opportunities, ANI also plans a 90-person expansion, which is tipped to be dedicated to acute gouty arthritis flares and unique to Cortrophin Gel. They also have plans to advance a Phase 4 clinical trial to strengthen the evidence base for the generation of Cortrophin Gel in patients with acute gouty arthritis flares.

Elsewhere, we’re seeing the company continue to drive pretty robust execution in the generics business. They’re leveraging operational expertise and seeing a lot of upsides from a U.S.-based manufacturing footprint to sustain cash flow. They also have a pretty disciplined capital allocation strategy to support both their rare disease and pipeline initiatives. Overall, I thought ANI’s outlook is very focused on sustained revenue growth with that ambitious guidance and also looking at a few areas of strategic investment in very high-value rare disease indications, which I'll get to.

Sales On Track

In terms of Purified Cortrophin Gel, we're seeing a pretty strong acceleration in sales momentum. And I see a clear line of sight toward ANI’s FY2026 guidance. In FY2025, Cortrophin Gel generated about $347.8 million in net revenues, so the guidance would be a 76% year-over-year jump. What's more is that 4Q25 sales are at $111.4 million, which would signal an 88% year-over-year jump. It tells me the annualized exit run rate is at about $445 million. So to reach the FY2026 guidance range midpoint at $557 million, Cortrophin Gel would need to grow an additional $95 to $130 million, which would be a 21% to 29% growth figure from the Q4 exit rate. So it's a step-down from 2025 growth that leaves a lot of upsides. Very achievable with the current sales trend, but of course, continued penetration in core specialties will be needed.

Iluvien and Yutiq, on the other hand, delivered $74.9 million in FY2025 revenues, with Iluvien alone guided at the $78 to $83 million range in FY2026. So on that front, they only need about a 4% to 11% growth in that area. That would suggest stabilization rather than outsized expansion. When combined, sustained Cortrophin Gel growth remains the primary driver of the revenue outlook, while modest Iluvien/Yutiq growth is set to give incremental support to hitting total company guidance of $1.055 to $1.115 billion.

High-Value Pipeline

As I mentioned, ANI, moving forward, wants to put more effort into high-value promising areas, and one of the biggest potential catalysts would be Cortrophin Gel. Particularly for patients experiencing acute gouty arthritis flares. So going forward, the company plans to initiate a Phase 4 clinical trial designed specifically to generate additional scientific evidence/clinical data supporting the efficacy and safety of Cortrophin Gel in that specific patient population. Now, the area already has a lot of supporting studies, but this Phase 4 trial seems to be needed to strengthen the clinical foundation for its use in acute gouty arthritis. This would be an indication unique to ANI in the adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) category, and give the added benefit of supporting broader adoption.

I see Cortrophin Gel specifically for patients experiencing acute gouty arthritis flares as a big driver of growth in the near term. You see, the condition is characterized by very sudden and severe joint pain. It often comes with swelling, redness, and inflammation, which are caused by the deposition of urate crystals in the joints. As you can imagine, the flares can be intensely painful and essentially limit your overall mobility. So the upcoming trial needs evidence that Cortrophin Gel can also be effective in this area. That is, rapidly reducing inflammation and pain during the episodes. Mechanistically speaking, the gel acts as an ACTH analogue, so it stimulates the adrenal cortex to increase endogenous cortisol production, which is used to modulate immune responses by providing a potent anti-inflammatory effect.

A Word On The TAM

If we take a look at the market for acute gouty arthritis flares, it’s actually pretty significant and growing, which is probably why ANI has decided to pursue it so aggressively. There are about 9 million adults in the U.S. who live with gout, which would be about 3.9% of the adult population. And a substantial proportion of these patients experience recurrent acute flare‑ups that would need immediate treatment. Industry estimates suggest that the acute gout flare therapeutics segment alone is valued somewhere in the low‑to‑mid-billions of dollars range annually in the U.S. The broader gout therapeutics market is also projected to grow to over $4 billion globally by 2030, and a sizeable portion is thought to be driven by acute care medications. In terms of the competition, current treatment options on the market would include NSAIDs, corticosteroids, and colchicine, with branded colchicine being quite popular, with its therapies historically generating hundreds of millions in annual sales. There are also biologic and novel agents entering development alongside the current standards of care.

Risk

The one risk I see here is that the company is expected to increase dependence on Purified Cortrophin Gel as the primary growth. The gel is actually expected to account for about 60% of total company revenues by 2026. So it means any disruption in the form of reimbursement or payer pushback, supply chain or even single-source manufacturing issues could have an outsized impact on the 2026 financial performance. This risk also gets somewhat amplified when you consider the company’s reliance on single-source third-party manufacturing for Cortrophin Gel and the fact that they need to successfully execute a 90-person Rare Disease commercial expansion at the same time.

Valuation

ANI valuation looks pretty attractively priced relative to both the sector and even its own historical averages. Now, despite the small run-up from post the 2026 outlook announcement, shares are still trading at a non-GAAP forward price to earnings of 11.2x, which would be a 43% discount to the sector median and a 24% discount to its 5-year average. Elsewhere, the forward EV to EBITDA ratio sits at 9.4x and is also meaningfully below peers. We’re also still seeing this discount despite ANI giving a strong earnings growth trajectory, with consensus EPS expected to rise about 45% in 2025 and continue growing through 2028. It’s set to drive the forward price to earnings down to 8.0x by 2028.

Other than GAAP multiples, which appear quite elevated due to amortization and acquisition-related impacts, their cash-flow and sales-based metrics also hint at an attractive valuation, with price to sales at 2.0x and EV to sales at 2.4x on a forward basis. As for cash, ANI managed to end 2025 with a pretty strong liquidity position of about $285 million in cash, and given that the company is now generating substantial positive operating cash flow. With an expected $275 to $290 million in adjusted non-GAAP EBITDA this year, it isn’t cash-burning. So they effectively have a multi-year cash runway to support that Phase 4 trial going forward.

data.title: Weekly Treasury Simulation, January 9, 2026: 50,000 No-Arbitrage Heath-Jarrow-Morton Yield Scenarios
data.description:
As explained in Prof. Robert Jarrow’s book cited below, forward rates contain a risk premium above and beyond the market’s expectations for the 3-month forward rate. We document the size of that risk premium in this graph, which shows the zero-coupon yield curve implied by current Treasury prices compared with the annualized compounded yield on 3-month Treasury bills that market participants would expect based on the daily movement of government bond yields in 14 countries since 1962. The risk premium, the reward for a long-term investment, is large and widens over most of the full 30-year maturity range. The graph also shows a decline in expected yields at a steady pace for the full 30 years. We explain the details below.

For more on this topic, see the analysis of government bond yields in 14 countries through November 30, 2025, given in the appendix.

Inverted Yields, Negative Rates, and U.S. Treasury Probabilities 10 Years Forward

The negative 2-year/10-year Treasury spread ended on August 26, 2024, after 537 trading days. The spread is currently at positive 0.64%. The table below shows that the August 26, 2024, streak of inverted yield curves is the longest in the U.S. Treasury market since the 2-year Treasury yield was first reported on June 1, 1976. The second longest streak is 423 trading days, starting on August 18, 1978.

In this week’s forecast, the focus is on three elements of interest rate behavior: the future probability of the recession-predicting inverted yield curve, the probability of negative rates, and the probability distribution of U.S. Treasury yields over the next decade.

We start from the closing U.S. Treasury yield curve published daily by the U.S. Department of the Treasury. Using a maximum smoothness forward rate approach, Friday’s implied forward rate curve shows the shortest maturity 1-month forward rates at 3.70%, versus 3.71% last week. There is a decline followed by a gradual rise until rates peak at 5.97%, compared to 6.03% the previous week. Forward rates stabilize at 4.68% at the 30-year horizon, versus 4.72% last week.

Using the methodology outlined in the appendix, we simulate 50,000 future paths for the U.S. Treasury yield curve out to thirty years. The next three sections summarize our conclusions from that simulation.

Inverted Treasury Yields: Positive Spread Now, 24.7% Probability by December 23, 2039

Many economists have concluded that a downward-sloping U.S. Treasury yield curve is an important indicator of future recessions. A recent example is this paper by Alex Domash and Lawrence H. Summers. We measure the probability that the 10-year par coupon Treasury yield is lower than the 2-year par coupon Treasury for every scenario in each of the first 40 quarterly periods in the simulation.[1] The next graph shows that the probability of an inverted yield now peaks at 24.7%, compared to 25.2% one week before, in the 91-day period ending December 23, 2039.

Negative Treasury Bill Yields: 11.2% Probability by December 13, 2047

The next graph describes the probability of negative 3-month Treasury bill rates for all but the first 3 months of the next three decades. The probability of negative rates starts near zero and then peaks at 11.2% in the 91-day period ending December 13, 2047.

Calculating the Default Risk from Interest Rate Maturity Mismatches

In light of the interest-rate-risk-driven failure of Silicon Valley Bank on March 10, 2023, we have added a table that applies equally well to banks, institutional investors, and individual investor mismatches from buying long-term Treasury bonds with borrowed short-term funds. We assume that the sole asset is a 10-year Treasury bond purchased at time zero at par value of $100. We analyze default risk for four different initial market value of equity to market value of asset ratios: 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%. For the banking example, we assume that the only class of liabilities is deposits that can be withdrawn at par at any time. In the institutional and retail investor case, we assume that the liability is essentially a borrowing on margin/repurchase agreement with the possibility of margin calls. For all investors, the amount of liabilities (95, 90, 85 or 80) represents a “strike price” on a put option held by the liability holders. Failure occurs via a margin call, bank run, or regulatory takeover (in the banking case) when the value of assets falls below the value of liabilities.

The graph below shows the cumulative 10-year probabilities of failure for each of the 4 possible capital ratios when the asset’s maturity is 10 years. For the 5 percent case, that default probability is 49.31%, compared to 49.80% from last week.

This default probability analysis is updated weekly based on the U.S. Treasury yield simulation described in the next section. The calculation process is the same for any portfolio of assets with credit risk included.

U.S. Treasury Probabilities 10 Years Forward

In this section, the focus turns to the decade ahead. This week’s simulation shows that the most likely range for the 3-month U.S. Treasury bill yield in ten years is from 1% to 2%, unchanged from last week. There is a 22.22% probability that the 3-month yield falls in this range, compared to 21.96% one week before. For the 10-year Treasury yield, the most likely range is from 2% to 3%, unchanged from last week. The probability of being in this range is 21.13%, compared to 20.80% one week prior.

In a recent post on SeekingAlpha, we pointed out that a forecast of “heads” or “tails” in a coin flip leaves out critical information. What a sophisticated bettor needs to know is that, on average for a fair coin, the probability of heads is 50%. A forecast that the next coin flip will be “heads” is literally worth nothing to investors because the outcome is purely random.

The same is true for interest rates.

In this section we present the detailed probability distribution for both the 3-month Treasury bill rate and the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield 10 years forward using semi-annual time steps.[2] We present the probability of where rates will be at each time step in one percent “rate buckets.” The forecast for 3-month Treasury yields is shown in this graph:

3-Month U.S. Treasury Yield Data Are Attached.

SAS3monthUST20260109.xlsx

The probability that the 3-month Treasury bill yield will be between 1% and 2% in 2 years is shown in column 4: 26.28%. The probability that the 3-month Treasury bill yield will be negative (as it has been often in Europe and Japan) in 2 years is 1.92% plus 0.06% plus 0.00% = 1.98% (difference due to rounding). Cells shaded in blue represent positive probabilities of occurring, but the probability has been rounded to the nearest 0.01%. The shading scheme is defined as follows:

  • Dark blue: the probability is greater than 0% but less than 1%
  • Light blue: the probability is greater than or equal to 1% and less than 5%
  • Light yellow: the probability is greater than or equal to 5% and 10%
  • Medium yellow: the probability is greater than or equal to 10% and less than 20%
  • Orange: the probability is greater than or equal to 20% and less than 25%
  • Red: the probability is greater than 25%

The chart below shows the same probabilities for the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield derived as part of the same simulation.

10-Year US Treasury Yield Data Are Attached.

SAS10yearUST20260109.xlsx

Modeling Correlated Foreign Exchange Rates and Government Yields

The SAS HJM++© methodology is based on five decades of research by Prof. Robert Jarrow. Using the no-arbitrage conditions of Heath, Jarrow, and Morton [1992] and foreign exchange rate no-arbitrage conditions from Amin and Jarrow [1992], foreign exchange rates and government yields in major markets are simulated jointly. The simulation is based on daily movements in government yields and foreign exchange rates since January 2, 1962. The resulting distribution of the yen-U.S. dollar exchange rate one year forward is shown in this graph:

Similarly, the forward-looking correlation between 10-year zero-coupon Bund and U.S. Treasury yields is shown below:

For more information on HJM++ scenario generation and no-arbitrage Monte Carlo simulation, please contact the author.

Appendix: Treasury Simulation Methodology

The Treasury yield probabilities are derived using the same methodology that SAS Institute Inc. recommends to its KRIS® and SAS ALM/Kamakura Risk Manager® clients. A moderately technical explanation is given later in the appendix, but we summarize it in plain English first.

Step 1: We take the closing U.S. Treasury yield curve as our starting point.

Step 2: We use the number of points on the yield curve that best explains historical yield curve shifts. Using daily government bond yield data from 14 countries from 1962 through November 30, 2025, we conclude that 12 “factors” drive almost all movements of government bond yields. The countries on which the analysis is based are Australia, Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, and New Zealand. Russia, Singapore, Spain, Sweden, Thailand, the United Kingdom, and the United States of America. No data from Russia is included after January 2022. The graph below shows which points on the zero-coupon yield curve are incorporated as risk factors and the order in which they are added to the model:

Step 3: We measure the volatility of changes in those factors and how volatility has changed over the same period.

Step 4: Using those measured volatilities, we generate 50,000 random shocks at each time step and derive the resulting yield curve.

Step 5: We “validate” the model to make sure that the simulation EXACTLY prices the starting Treasury curve and that it fits history as well as possible. The methodology for doing this is described below.

Step 6: We take all 50,000 simulated yield curves and calculate the probabilities that yields fall in each of the 1% “buckets” displayed in the graph.

Do Treasury Yields Accurately Reflect Expected Future Inflation?

We showed in a recent post on SeekingAlpha that, on average, investors have almost always done better by buying long-term bonds than by rolling over short-term Treasury bills. That means that market participants have generally (but not always) been accurate in forecasting future inflation and adding a risk premium to that forecast.

The distribution above helps investors estimate the probability of success from going long.

Finally, as mentioned weekly in the Corporate Bond Investor Friday overview, the future expenses (both the amount and the timing) that all investors are trying to cover with their investments are an important part of investment strategy. The author follows his own advice: cover the short-term cash needs first and then step out to cover more distant cash needs as savings and investment returns accumulate.

Technical Details

Daily government bond yields from the 14 countries listed above form the base historical data for fitting the number of yield curve factors and their volatility. The U.S. historical data is provided by the U.S. Department of the Treasury. The use of the international bond data increases the number of observations to more than 109,000 and provides a more complete range of experience with both high rates and negative rates than a U.S. data set alone provides.

The modeling process was published in a very important paper by David Heath, Robert Jarrow, and Andrew Morton in 1992:

For technically inclined readers, we recommend Prof. Jarrow’s book Modeling Fixed Income Securities and Interest Rate Options for those who want to know exactly how the “HJM” model construction works.

The number of factors, 12 for the 14-country model, has been stable since June 30, 2017.

Footnotes:

[1] After the first 20 years in the simulation, the 10-year Treasury cannot be derived from the initial 30 years of Treasury yields.

[2] The full simulation uses 91-day time steps for 30 years forward. This note summarizes just the first 10 years of the full simulation.

data.title: Postal Realty Trust: Growth, High Dividends, And Low Valuation
data.description:
I'm initiating coverage of Postal Realty Trust (PSTL), which I see as a steady growth REIT with a strong dividend yield. The REIT's low valuation leaves more room to the upside for the stock.

Postal Realty Trust Business Background

Postal Realty Trust operates as a $545 million market cap New York-based realty investment trust [REIT]. The REIT is focused on acquiring/managing properties that are mostly leased to the United States Postal Service [USPS]. This includes postal industrial facilities and post offices. As of September 30, 2025, PSTL owned a portfolio of 1,853 properties. However, PSTL just acquired 65 additional properties leased to USPS on January 8, 2026. The 65 properties were purchased for $29.1 million and they comprise 142k of net leasable interior square feet. This brings PSTL's total portfolio of properties up to 1,917 for about $7.1 million of leasable interior square feet.

PSTL obtains its revenue from rent and tenant reimbursements. These are through leases with the USPS and from fees from managing the properties.

As a REIT, Postal Realty Trust doesn't pay Federal income tax on the net taxable income that is paid to shareholders. PSTL is required to pay a minimum of 90% of taxable income to shareholders under the REIT code. PSTL currently has an annual dividend yield of about 6%.

Postal Realty Trust's Growth Catalysts

Postal Realty has multiple factors that act as growth catalysts. PSTL has a three main strategies that it employs to drive growth and to reward shareholders. The first strategy is to drive organic growth within the REIT's portfolio. This is accomplished by "programmatic leasing with the Postal Service." This involves the process of negotiating new leases for the existing owned properties and for future acquisitions.

The 2nd strategy involves sourcing and acquiring postal properties that can be immediately accretive to the bottom line. PSTL also targets properties that can "become significantly more accretive over time." Postal Realty's 3rd strategy involves "increasing access to capital to fund the accretive growth."

These strategies led PSTL to achieve a 23% yoy revenue increase to $24.3 million. This exceeded Postal Realty's expectations. The results were attributed to "faster lease executions with the USPS" and from fees/other income exceeding expectations.

PSTL also achieved a 10% yoy increase in AFFO to $0.33 per share in Q3 2025. Postal Realty increased its AFFO (adjusted funds from operations) per share guidance for 2025 by $0.06 to a range of $1.30 to $1.32. This would result in 13% annual AFFO growth. PSTL believes that it has good visibility for revenue growth for 2026 and will provide that guidance in the Q4 2025 earnings call scheduled for February 27, 2026.

Postal Realty is guiding total 2025 acquisitions to meet or exceed $110 million. PSTL makes acquisitions when the cost of capital makes sense. This strategy is what enables PSTL's acquisitions to be immediately accretive. Once an acquisition is made, PSTL strives to "increase value to the Postal Service as an efficient point of contact." At the same time, PSTL drives organic growth for shareholders by discovering good lease opportunities with higher annual rent potential.

PSTL also decreased operating expenses by 2.7% from Q3 2024 to Q3 2025. This led to the operating margin to increase from 25.4% to 37.9% over the same time. This was a result of "the timing and scope of repairs and maintenance projects."

Strong Balance Sheet/Cash Flow

One of the reasons why the REIT is successful is because of PSTL's strong balance sheet. At the end of Q3 2025, "net debt to annualized adjusted EBITDA was 5.2x." The weighted average debt maturity was 3.5 years while the fixed rate debt comprised 93% of PSTL's borrowings.

PSTL has total assets of $735 million and total liabilities of $387 million for total equity of $348.5 million. Postal Realty's total buildings have a worth of $581 million. PSTL also has land worth about $158 million. With an asset to liability ratio of about 1.9x, PSTL is in great shape to handle its debt obligations.

Adding to the balance sheet strength is PSTL's strong cash flow. PSTL had $42.4 million in operating cash flow over the trailing 12 months. Postal Realty increased its operating cash flow every year since at least 2017. The operating cash flow increased 36% yoy to $10.9 million in Q3 2025. This strong cash flow growth puts PSTL in great shape to continue its real estate acquisitions.

Valuation

I'm using the price/AFFO ratio since it is a standard valuation metric for REITs. Postal Realty Trust is trading with a forward price/AFFO of 12.4. This is below the sector median of 15.4. So, the REIT is trading with a slight undervaluation.

PSTL is expected to grow revenue at 16.5% to $106 million and FFO per share at 5.3% to $1.36 in 2026 according to analysts' consensus estimates. PSTL has been growing revenue at double-digit rates for multiple years. This is expected to continue over the next few years. Postal Realty's strategy of acquiring new properties for rental income should continue to drive this strong growth.

The chart above shows how PSTL's price has been lagging its FFO over the past few years. This tells me that the price has some catching up to do. I expect the REIT's price to rise steadily in 2026 as PSTL continues its growth from this low valuation level.

Technical Perspective

Postal Realty's weekly chart shows a strong uptrend. The REIT is bullish as the RSI remains above the 50 level. The MACD confirms the bullish trend as the blue line is above the red signal line while the histogram bars turned green.

While the stock doesn't go up in a straight line with pullbacks along the way, I expect the overall upward trend to continue in 2026 and beyond. PSTL has a solid strategy for sustainable growth over the long-term. This should drive strong revenue and cash flow growth, which should drive the stock higher.

The Q4 earnings report scheduled for February 27, 2026 could be the next strong catalyst for Postal Realty. News of property acquisitions could be another catalyst.

Risks for Postal Realty Trust

Postal Realty Trust highly depends on the demand for leased postal properties. PSTL derives all of its revenue from properties that are leased to the USPS. Decreases in the demand for these properties would have a significant negative impact on PSTL's revenue and AFFO. Demand for postal properties could change due to changes in government spending, increased competition from other delivery companies, etc.

The USPS has a ten-year plan to address the shift from letter delivery to package delivery. Issues to be addressed include: "the declining financial situation, processing underperformance, retail/delivery operations, and transportation." It is possible that this plan could negatively impact PSTL if USPS stopped renewing some of its leases that are owned by the REIT.

It is possible that PSTL fails to acquire new properties at the pace that it intends to over the long-term. This could lead to less than expected growth and negatively impact revenue, AFFO, and the stock price. The amount of properties that are leased to the USPS is limited. So, PSTL could run into challenges with its acquisition plans.

Postal Realty Trust's Long-Term Outlook

Postal Realty has a positive long-term growth outlook. The REIT has a sound strategy to acquire and lease USPS properties. So far, there is no other publicly traded REIT that does this. PSTL has a great multiple-year track record of growing revenue at an above-average double-digit annual pace. This is expected to continue in 2026 and beyond.

I expect PSTL's strong revenue growth, margin expansion, and AFFO growth to continue to drive the stock higher at an above-average pace. The low valuation level should help support above-average gains. The attractive dividend yield of 6% is also likely to be supported by this growth. PSTL increased its dividends for six straight years since its IPO in 2019. So, I expect this track record to continue.

data.title: Under Armour: A Bad Bet As Market Share Issues Deepen
data.description:
So far, in early 2026, markets are fighting off all bearish triggers. Despite overheated valuations, uncertain macroeconomic footing, and the latest tussle between the White House and Fed Chair Powell, the S&P 500 remains trading near all-time highs.

But this isn’t a time to be complacent. In my view, visual stock picking is more important than ever, and we have to be especially careful to avoid stocks that are losing the most market share in their industries. Under Armour, Inc. (UA) is particularly at risk. The stock is down nearly 30% over the past year, but in December, experienced a ~20% burst from lows that I consider to be a phantom rally with weak fundamental justification.

I’m initiating Under Armour at a "Sell" rating.

Weak Sales Data That Reflects Waning Market Share

As most investors are aware, Under Armour was founded 30 years ago in 1996. Its defining product, and what customers still associate the brand with today, is a sweat-wicking, mesh material that has since become more or less the standard for athletic shirts and performance apparel.

But unfortunately, Under Armour has fallen from being one of the most premium brands in sportswear to an afterthought. The top item on the bear case for Under Armour is persistently weak sales performance. The company’s fiscal Q2 (September quarter) earnings results are shown below:

Under Armour’s revenue in Q2 fell 5% y/y to $1.33 billion, which worsened slightly from -4% growth in Q1.

We note that while Under Armour is showcasing growth and a revival in Europe, its largest market in the U.S. has declined 8% y/y. Meanwhile, Asia, which was one of the company’s most meaningful growth drivers, fell 14% y/y.

There’s an element of wholesale retraction here as well. Wholesale revenue fell 6% y/y, underperforming direct-to-consumer revenue of -2%. This is an indication that retailers are wary of taking on slow-moving inventory in a tougher macro climate.

Where we are particularly concerned, however, is Under Armour’s loss of market share, which can be implied from recent trends. We note that for all the fanfare around Nike’s recent fall from grace, Nike is actually faring a lot better than Under Armour. The latest results from Nike (through the November quarter) show 1% overall growth, with 4% growth in apparel. Apparel is the most direct comparison for Under Armour, as its presence in footwear is smaller relative to its clothing line.

It’s not just Nike, either. Again, Lululemon (LULU) has been cited to be losing share, particularly among the core U.S. women’s demographic, and yet the Canadian athletic giant is still showing better results than Under Armour. Lululemon grew 7% in the most recent quarter, especially driven by 47% currency-neutral growth in mainland China, its fastest-growing region. Meanwhile, Under Armour counts Asia as its fastest-declining region.

In more recent news, Lululemon also cited strong holiday sales for the fourth quarter and lifted its operating margin guidance. All of these are clear indicators that Under Armour is losing share.

Adding insult to injury, Under Armour’s gross margins are falling. In Q3, gross margin of 47.3% fell 250bps y/y. Now, of course, there’s an element of tariff impact in this comparison. Across the board, Nike and Lululemon are also seeing margins peel away.

But the bigger concern to me is that falling margins across the whole space indicate that the sector is also having to lean more deeply into promotions to keep customers satisfied. Among its competitors, Under Armour is the weakest positioned to absorb a profit hit.

Not Doing Enough On Cost To Adjust To Its Weaker Reality

To me, the nail in the coffin for Under Armour is that the company isn’t reacting quickly enough or deeply enough to adjust to its slower sales reality.

Fads come and go in the apparel space. Once momentum is lost, it’s very difficult to reclaim. We only have to look at the deep graveyard of fallen apparel and retail stocks to confirm that reality. But it’s much harder to peel back a corporate bureaucracy, once it’s been built.

In Q3, Under Armour’s selling, general, and administrative expenses grew at 12% y/y, and even excluding $4 million of one-time payments bucketed here for restructuring, the company’s adjusted SG&A would still have grown 9% y/y. Further adjusting for a $27 million positive insurance recovery last year, the company notes SG&A would have been roughly flat. Still, it’s not good enough.

In 2025, Under Armour announced a broad restructuring plan. Updating investors on the latest on this plan, the company notes it achieved $35 million of run rate cost savings in 2025, and is on track to achieve $45 million more in 2026. Per CFO David Bergman’s remarks on the most recent earnings call:

Since the plan's inception, we have incurred $147 million in charges and transformation expenses, of which $82 million are cash related and $65 million are noncash. We expect total charges and expenses for the plan to be up to $160 million, which will be recognized by the end of fiscal 2026. The actions executed under our plan have already delivered approximately $35 million in savings in fiscal 2025 and are on track to generate an additional $45 million in fiscal '26.

We think spending $160 million in one-time charges to achieve $80 million of annual savings, with a payback period of more than two years considering the linearity of savings achievement is back-end loaded, is not entirely effective. Also, when we consider Under Armour’s $5 billion in annualized revenue, $80 million of annualized savings translates to less than a 2% improvement in operating margins.

In my view, Under Armour should be cutting costs at a much deeper pace to reflect its 5% y/y revenue decline and market share losses. Perhaps the fact that the company isn’t cutting deeper is a reflection that it’s unable to, and its structural profitability at its shrunken revenue scale is much weaker.

Valuation And Key Takeaways

Despite a company in crisis, Under Armour still sits at what I consider to be an unrealistic valuation multiple.

As shown above, Under Armour's ~15x forward EBITDA multiple is cheaper only than Nike (NKE), which has retained its legacy premium. Still, Lululemon is trading substantially cheaper despite recent growth trends, and beyond sports and performance apparel, there are other names like Gap (GAP) and Urban Outfitters (URBN) trading even cheaper.

Steer clear here and invest elsewhere. With Under Armour’s share losses and weak attempts at cost mitigation, its recent uptrend isn’t likely to be sustained very long.
